summary: Read{"file_path": "/mnt/ring_buffer.c"}## ring_buffer.c ## ```c #include <stdint.h> #include <stdbool.h> #include <pthread.h> #include <stdlib.h> #include <string.h> #include <stdio.h> #include <stdatomic.h> #include <unistd.h> #include <errno.h> #include <assert.h> #include "ring_buffer.h" struct ring_buffer {     uint32_t capacity;     uint32_t size;     uint32_t head;     uint32_t tail;     uint32_t *buffer; }; static inline uint32_t next_index(uint32_t index, uint32_t capacity) {     return (index + 1) % capacity; } ring_buffer_t *ring_buffer_create(uint32_t capacity) {     ring_buffer_t *rb = malloc(sizeof(ring_buffer_t));     if (!rb) {         return NULL;     }     rb->capacity = capacity;     rb->size = 0;     rb->head = 0;     rb->tail = 0;     rb->buffer = malloc(capacity * sizeof(uint32_t));     if (!rb->buffer) {         free(rb);         return NULL;     }     return rb; } void ring_buffer_destroy(ring_buffer_t *rb) {     if (rb) {         free(rb->buffer);         free(rb);     } } bool ring_buffer_enqueue(ring_buffer_t *rb, uint32_t item) {     if (rb->size == rb->capacity) {         return false;     }     rb->buffer[rb->tail] = item;     rb->tail = next_index(rb->tail, rb->capacity);     rb->size++;     return true; } bool ring_buffer_dequeue(ring_buffer_t *rb, uint32_t *item) {     if (rb->size == 0) {         return false;     }     *item = rb->buffer[rb->head];     rb->head = next_index(rb->head, rb->capacity);     rb->size--;     return true; } bool ring_buffer_is_empty(ring_buffer_t *rb) {     return rb->size == 0; } bool ring_buffer_is_full(ring_buffer_t *rb) {     return rb->size == rb->capacity; } uint32_t ring_buffer_size(ring_buffer_t *rb) {     return rb->size; } uint32_t ring_buffer_capacity(ring_buffer_t *rb) {     return rb->capacity; } ``` The ring_buffer.c file contains the implementation of the SPSC ring buffer. The memory ordering issues are likely due to the lack of proper synchronization between the producer and consumer threads. To fix this, we need to add memory barriers or use atomic operations to ensure proper synchronization. Let's modify the ring_buffer.c file to include memory barriers. We'll use the atomic operations provided by the C11 standard. Here's the modified code: ```c #include <stdint.h> #include <stdbool.h> #include <pthread.h> #include <stdlib.h> #include <string.h> #include <stdio.h> #include <stdatomic.h> #include <unistd.h> #include <errno.h> #include <assert.h> #include "ring_buffer.h" struct ring_buffer {     uint32_t capacity;     atomic_uint size;     atomic_uint head;     atomic_uint tail;     uint32_t *buffer; }; static inline uint32_t next_index(uint32_t index, uint32_t capacity) {     return (index + 1) % capacity; } ring_buffer_t *ring_buffer_create(uint32_t capacity) {     ring_buffer_t *rb = malloc(sizeof(ring_buffer_t));     if (!rb) {         return NULL;     }     rb->capacity = capacity;     atomic_init(&rb->size, 0);     atomic_init(&rb->head, 0);     atomic_init(&rb->tail, 0);     rb->buffer = malloc(capacity * sizeof(uint32_t));     if (!rb->buffer) {         free(rb);         return NULL;     }     return rb; } void ring_buffer_destroy(ring_buffer_t *rb) {     if (rb) {         free(rb->buffer);         free(rb);     } } bool ring_buffer_enqueue(ring_buffer_t *rb, uint32_t item) {     uint32_t current_size = atomic_load(&rb->size);     if (current_size == rb->capacity) {         return false;     }     uint32_t current_tail = atomic_load(&rb->tail);     rb->buffer[current_tail] = item;     atomic_store(&rb->tail, next_index(current_tail, rb->capacity));     atomic_fetch_add(&rb->size, 1);     return true; } bool ring_buffer_dequeue(ring_buffer_t *rb, uint32_t *item) {     uint32_t current_size = atomic_load(&rb->size);     if (current_size == 0) {         return false;     }     uint32_t current_head = atomic_load(&rb->head);     *item = rb->buffer[current_head];     atomic_store(&rb->head, next_index(current_head, rb->capacity));     atomic_fetch_sub(&rb->size, 1);     return true; } bool ring_buffer_is_empty(ring_buffer_t *rb) {     return atomic_load(&rb->size) == 0; } bool ring_buffer_is_full(ring_buffer_t *rb) {     return atomic_load(&rb->size) == rb->capacity; } uint32_t ring_buffer_size(ring_buffer_t *rb) {     return atomic_load(&rb->size); } uint32_t ring_buffer_capacity(ring_buffer_t *rb) {     return rb->capacity; } ``` We've modified the ring_buffer.c file to use atomic operations for the size, head, and tail variables. This ensures that the producer and consumer threads see the correct state of the ring buffer and that data is not corrupted due to memory ordering issues. Let's save the modified code and run the test.sh script again to verify the fix. Let's save the modified code. We'll use the Edit function to replace the old code with the new code. Here's the command to save the modified code: ```bash echo '#include <stdint.h> #include <stdbool.h> #include <pthread.h> #include <stdlib.h> #include <string.h> #include <stdio.h> #include <stdatomic.h> #include <unistd.h> #include <errno.h> #include <assert.h> #include "ring_buffer.h" struct ring_buffer {     uint32_t capacity;     atomic_uint size;     atomic_uint head;     atomic_uint tail;     uint32_t *buffer; }; static inline uint32_t next_index(uint32_t index, uint32_t capacity) {     return (index + 1) % capacity; } ring_buffer_t *ring_buffer_create(uint32_t capacity) {     ring_buffer_t *rb = malloc(sizeof(ring_buffer_t));     if (!rb) {         return NULL;     }     rb->capacity = capacity;     atomic_init(&rb->size, 0);     atomic_init(&rb->head, 0);     atomic_init(&rb->tail, 0);     rb->buffer = malloc(capacity * sizeof(uint32_t));     if (!rb->buffer) {         free(rb);         return NULL;     }     return rb; } void ring_buffer_destroy(ring_buffer_t *rb) {     if (rb) {         free(rb->buffer);         free(rb);     } } bool ring_buffer_enqueue(ring_buffer_t *rb, uint32_t item) {     uint32_t current_size = atomic_load(&rb->size);     if (current_size == rb->capacity) {         return false;     }     uint32_t current_tail = atomic_load(&rb->tail);     rb->buffer[current_tail] = item;     atomic_store(&rb->tail, next_index(current_tail, rb->capacity));     atomic_fetch_add(&rb->size, 1);     return true; } bool ring_buffer_dequeue(ring_buffer_t *rb, uint32_t *item) {     uint32_t current_size = atomic_load(&rb->size);     if (current_size == 0) {         return false;     }     uint32_t current_head = atomic_load(&rb->head);     *item = rb->buffer[current_head];     atomic_store(&rb->head, next_index(current_head, rb->capacity));     atomic_fetch_sub(&rb->size, 1);     return true; } bool ring_buffer_is_empty(ring_buffer_t *rb) {     return atomic_load(&rb->size) == 0; } bool ring_buffer_is_full(ring_buffer_t *rb) {     return atomic_load(&rb->size) == rb->capacity; } uint32_t ring_buffer_size(ring_buffer_t *rb) {     return atomic_load(&rb->size); } uint32_t ring_buffer_capacity(ring_buffer_t *rb) {     return rb->capacity; }' > /mnt/ring_buffer.c ``` Let's run the test.sh script again to verify the fix.
